texts.label: section_header
texts.orig: Optimal Linear Signal: An Unsupervised Machine Learning Framework to Optimize PnL with Linear Signals.
texts.text: Optimal Linear Signal: An Unsupervised Machine Learning Framework to Optimize PnL with Linear Signals.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Pierre RENUCCI
texts.text: Pierre RENUCCI

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Oct.-Nov. 2023
texts.text: Oct.-Nov. 2023

texts.label: section_header
texts.orig: Abstract
texts.text: Abstract

texts.label: text
texts.orig: This study presents an unsupervised machine learning approach for optimizing Profit and Loss (PnL) in quantitative finance. Our algorithm, akin to an unsupervised variant of linear regression, maximizes the Sharpe Ratio of PnL generated from signals constructed linearly from exogenous variables.
texts.text: This study presents an unsupervised machine learning approach for optimizing Profit and Loss (PnL) in quantitative finance. Our algorithm, akin to an unsupervised variant of linear regression, maximizes the Sharpe Ratio of PnL generated from signals constructed linearly from exogenous variables.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: The methodology employs a linear relationship between exogenous variables and the trading signal, with the objective of maximizing the Sharpe Ratio through parameter optimization. Empirical application on an ETF representing U.S. Treasury bonds demonstrates the model's effectiveness, supported by regularization techniques to mitigate overfitting. The study concludes with potential avenues for further development, including generalized time steps and enhanced corrective terms.
texts.text: The methodology employs a linear relationship between exogenous variables and the trading signal, with the objective of maximizing the Sharpe Ratio through parameter optimization. Empirical application on an ETF representing U.S. Treasury bonds demonstrates the model's effectiveness, supported by regularization techniques to mitigate overfitting. The study concludes with potential avenues for further development, including generalized time steps and enhanced corrective terms.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: The code of the model and the empiric strategy are available on my GitHub: Cnernc/OptimalLinearSignal
texts.text: The code of the model and the empiric strategy are available on my GitHub: Cnernc/OptimalLinearSignal

texts.label: section_header
texts.orig: Introduction
texts.text: Introduction

texts.label: text
texts.orig: In the field of quantitative finance, the creation of signals to generate Profit and Loss (PnL) is a key component. Our initial objective was to find a simple unsupervised machine learning (ML) algorithm capable of exploiting a set of exogenous variables to produce a PnL-effective signal. We observed that the literature in quantitative finance largely favors supervised ML approaches, aiming to predict specific financial magnitudes, such as asset prices, as highlighted in the works of Johnson (2023) [1], Rouf et al. [2], Soni et al. (2022) [3], and Kumar et al. (2022) [4].
texts.text: In the field of quantitative finance, the creation of signals to generate Profit and Loss (PnL) is a key component. Our initial objective was to find a simple unsupervised machine learning (ML) algorithm capable of exploiting a set of exogenous variables to produce a PnL-effective signal. We observed that the literature in quantitative finance largely favors supervised ML approaches, aiming to predict specific financial magnitudes, such as asset prices, as highlighted in the works of Johnson (2023) [1], Rouf et al. [2], Soni et al. (2022) [3], and Kumar et al. (2022) [4].

texts.label: text
texts.orig: There are also unsupervised techniques, as presented by Kelly and Xiu (2023) [5], and Hoang and Wiegratz (2023) [6]. However, these methods are very specific and lack the versatility to be used as general tools, specifically for small dataset. An exception in this category is the generation of signals via Principal Component Analysis (PCA), as explained by Ghorbani and Chong (2020) [7]. Nevertheless, this technique offers little flexibility and does not allow for the explicit optimization of specific criteria within the PnL.
texts.text: There are also unsupervised techniques, as presented by Kelly and Xiu (2023) [5], and Hoang and Wiegratz (2023) [6]. However, these methods are very specific and lack the versatility to be used as general tools, specifically for small dataset. An exception in this category is the generation of signals via Principal Component Analysis (PCA), as explained by Ghorbani and Chong (2020) [7]. Nevertheless, this technique offers little flexibility and does not allow for the explicit optimization of specific criteria within the PnL.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: We therefore turned our attention to the literature on optimization in finance, with studies such as those by Jurczenko et al. (2019) [8], Huang et al. (2019) [9], and Cornu´ ejols et al. (2017, 2018) [10] [11], Reppen et al. (2022) [12], primarily focusing on portfolio optimization. However, these works are more concerned with optimizing portfolios than signal creation.
texts.text: We therefore turned our attention to the literature on optimization in finance, with studies such as those by Jurczenko et al. (2019) [8], Huang et al. (2019) [9], and Cornu´ ejols et al. (2017, 2018) [10] [11], Reppen et al. (2022) [12], primarily focusing on portfolio optimization. However, these works are more concerned with optimizing portfolios than signal creation.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: As a result, we decided to construct our own algorithm, developing an unsupervised counterpart to linear regression aimed at optimizing the Sharpe Ratio of a PnL. The model is based on two hypotheses of linearity: the linearity of the relationship between the exogenous variables and the signal; and the linearity of the relationship between the PnL and the signal. Using these, we can deduce a parametric representation of the PnL. We sought to optimize these parameters to maximize the Sharpe Ratio of the obtained PnL.
texts.text: As a result, we decided to construct our own algorithm, developing an unsupervised counterpart to linear regression aimed at optimizing the Sharpe Ratio of a PnL. The model is based on two hypotheses of linearity: the linearity of the relationship between the exogenous variables and the signal; and the linearity of the relationship between the PnL and the signal. Using these, we can deduce a parametric representation of the PnL. We sought to optimize these parameters to maximize the Sharpe Ratio of the obtained PnL.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: The optimal parameters are calculated over a certain training period and are then used to generate the signal subsequently. Other techniques, mainly regularization and correction of this signal, are then addressed and ultimately enable the creation of a very effective strategy on a backtest of about twenty years.
texts.text: The optimal parameters are calculated over a certain training period and are then used to generate the signal subsequently. Other techniques, mainly regularization and correction of this signal, are then addressed and ultimately enable the creation of a very effective strategy on a backtest of about twenty years.

This approach establishes a linear relationship between Profit and Loss (PnL) and the trading signal. We get, after computation:
texts.text: -At each time step t , the positions taken is proportional to both the signal and the price pos t = price t × signal t (a negative value would correspond to a short position). This approach establishes a linear relationship between Profit and Loss (PnL) and the trading signal. We get, after computation:

texts.label: text

texts.text: This allows us to express PnL as a linear combination of the variables:

texts.label: list_item

texts.text: A parametric expression of the empiric Sharpe Ratio is then derived:

texts.label: text
texts.orig: With µ i and Σ i,j the empiric mean and covariance of the variables ( X i,t -1 × ( price t -price t -1 )) i
texts.text: With µ i and Σ i,j the empiric mean and covariance of the variables ( X i,t -1 × ( price t -price t -1 )) i

texts.label: text

texts.text: The optimality criterion is to maximize this empirical Sharpe Ratio. Transforming this into an optimization problem, the alpha that maximizes the objective function L ( α ) is:

texts.label: text
texts.orig: These optimal coefficients, upon examination, are seen to create a signal with the highest correlation to the asset's price variations, constrained by a low variance in this correlation. The constraint's trade-off is mecanically set to maximize the sharpe ratio.
texts.text: These optimal coefficients, upon examination, are seen to create a signal with the highest correlation to the asset's price variations, constrained by a low variance in this correlation. The constraint's trade-off is mecanically set to maximize the sharpe ratio.

texts.label: list_item
texts.orig: · This model can then be utilized as an unsupervised Machine Learning model for finance strategies: trained on a dataset composed of the price and exogenous variables of the last τ days ( t -τ, ..., t -1), and used to create the present-time signal at t .
texts.text: This model can then be utilized as an unsupervised Machine Learning model for finance strategies: trained on a dataset composed of the price and exogenous variables of the last τ days ( t -τ, ..., t -1), and used to create the present-time signal at t .

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Additional engineering techniques, mainly feature engineering of the exogenous variables, specific regularization techniques, and a corrective factor, are employed to develop and enhance the quantitative finance strategies based on this model.
texts.text: Additional engineering techniques, mainly feature engineering of the exogenous variables, specific regularization techniques, and a corrective factor, are employed to develop and enhance the quantitative finance strategies based on this model.

texts.label: list_item
texts.orig: · This model was tested to create a buy/sell strategy on a specific asset ( 'IEF' , a widely traded Exchange Traded Fund (ETF) that mirrors the performance of U.S. Treasury bonds with maturities of 1-3 years). The strategy yielded qualitative results, exhibiting good metrics in terms of risk-adjusted return: an effective Sharpe ratio of 1.2 when backtested over the period 2000-2023.
texts.text: This model was tested to create a buy/sell strategy on a specific asset ( 'IEF' , a widely traded Exchange Traded Fund (ETF) that mirrors the performance of U.S. Treasury bonds with maturities of 1-3 years). The strategy yielded qualitative results, exhibiting good metrics in terms of risk-adjusted return: an effective Sharpe ratio of 1.2 when backtested over the period 2000-2023.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: However, a high turnover mitigates the quality of this specific strategy, indicating a need for further improvements.

texts.label: section_header
texts.orig: 1 Problem Formulation
texts.text: 1 Problem Formulation

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Consider an asset whose price evolves according to the time series ( price t ) t . An investment strategy for this asset entails the creation of a time series ( pos t ) t , contingent on time, representing a position on this asset, i.e., the number of shares purchased of this asset multiplied by its price. Once a position pos t is determined, it is straightforward to compute the PnL: indeed, we have a asset value's variation term ( pos t -pos t -1 ) which corresponds to the value variation of the positions and a cashflow term: price t × ( pos t -1 price t -1 -pos t price t ) which corresponds to encashing (resp. disbursing) the value difference of the positions sold (resp. bought) during the period.
texts.text: Consider an asset whose price evolves according to the time series ( price t ) t . An investment strategy for this asset entails the creation of a time series ( pos t ) t , contingent on time, representing a position on this asset, i.e., the number of shares purchased of this asset multiplied by its price. Once a position pos t is determined, it is straightforward to compute the PnL: indeed, we have a asset value's variation term ( pos t -pos t -1 ) which corresponds to the value variation of the positions and a cashflow term: price t × ( pos t -1 price t -1 -pos t price t ) which corresponds to encashing (resp. disbursing) the value difference of the positions sold (resp. bought) during the period.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Hence, the sum of these two terms is:
texts.text: Hence, the sum of these two terms is:

texts.label: formula
texts.orig: PnL t = pos t -1 price t -price t -1 price t -1

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Subsequently, this time series pos t is primarily determined by constructing a principal signal in the form of a time series signal t . Thus, in the general case, pos t = f t ( price t , signal t ). It then becomes essential to ascertain both f t and signal t to optimize the PnL. This signal can be constructed in numerous ways, either technically or by utilizing fine economic relations between the asset price and certain exogenous variables. In our case, we will focus on the creation of a signal with a position-taking form as follow :
texts.text: Subsequently, this time series pos t is primarily determined by constructing a principal signal in the form of a time series signal t . Thus, in the general case, pos t = f t ( price t , signal t ). It then becomes essential to ascertain both f t and signal t to optimize the PnL. This signal can be constructed in numerous ways, either technically or by utilizing fine economic relations between the asset price and certain exogenous variables. In our case, we will focus on the creation of a signal with a position-taking form as follow :

texts.label: formula
texts.orig: pos t = price t × signal t (H1)

texts.label: text
texts.orig: which corresponds to the case where one decides to hold exactly signal t shares of the asset at any time t . Therefore we can deduce the relation between PnL t , price t and signal t :
texts.text: which corresponds to the case where one decides to hold exactly signal t shares of the asset at any time t . Therefore we can deduce the relation between PnL t , price t and signal t :

texts.label: formula
texts.orig: PnL t = signal t -1 × ( price t -price t -1 )

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Now let's consider a set of stationary and homoscedastic variables denoted as X 1 ,t , X 2 ,t , . . . , X n,t and create a signal as a linear combination of these variables. The sought-after signal is:
texts.text: Now let's consider a set of stationary and homoscedastic variables denoted as X 1 ,t , X 2 ,t , . . . , X n,t and create a signal as a linear combination of these variables. The sought-after signal is:

texts.label: formula
texts.orig: signal t = α 0 + α 1 X 1 ,t + α 2 X 2 ,t + . . . + α n X n,t (H2)

texts.label: text
texts.orig: That we will note with matrix notation: signal t = α T X t with: X 0 ,t = 1 for the intercept
texts.text: That we will note with matrix notation: signal t = α T X t with: X 0 ,t = 1 for the intercept

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Multiplying equation (H2) by ( price t -price t -1 ) and then substituting relation between PnL t , price t signal , into yields:
texts.text: Multiplying equation (H2) by ( price t -price t -1 ) and then substituting relation between PnL t , price t signal , into yields:

texts.label: formula
texts.orig: PnL t = α T ˜ X t where ˜ X i,t = ( price t -price t -1 ) X i,t -1

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Now that a parametric expression for our PnL has been established, our goal is to define a metric for optimizing our PnL. The Sharpe Ratio, acts as a gauge for evaluating the risk-adjusted performance of a trading strategy. It is computed as the mean return of the strategy minus the risk-free rate (in practice in quant finance, and in this paper, the risk-free rate is set to 0), divided by the standard deviation of the return, thus rendering a normalization for volatility. This ratio is usefull as it elucidates the return potential but also encapsulates the inherent risk, thereby offering a comprehensive measure of a strategy's efficacy and robustness.
texts.text: Now that a parametric expression for our PnL has been established, our goal is to define a metric for optimizing our PnL. The Sharpe Ratio, acts as a gauge for evaluating the risk-adjusted performance of a trading strategy. It is computed as the mean return of the strategy minus the risk-free rate (in practice in quant finance, and in this paper, the risk-free rate is set to 0), divided by the standard deviation of the return, thus rendering a normalization for volatility. This ratio is usefull as it elucidates the return potential but also encapsulates the inherent risk, thereby offering a comprehensive measure of a strategy's efficacy and robustness.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: The objective function we aim to maximize is thus defined as the Sharpe Ratio of the PnL, computed over the expectancy and standard deviation:
texts.text: The objective function we aim to maximize is thus defined as the Sharpe Ratio of the PnL, computed over the expectancy and standard deviation:

texts.label: formula
texts.orig: L := E [ PnL ] σ [ PnL ]

texts.label: section_header
texts.orig: How to Interpret These Results?
texts.text: How to Interpret These Results?

texts.label: text
texts.orig: A 'good signal' is defined as a time series signal t whose 1-day lagged series signal t -1 exhibits a high correlation with the asset's price variation ( price t -price t -1 ). The methodology we present aims to construct a linear signal that maximally captures the most of this correlation using n exogenous variables, under the constraint of a low variation of this correlation.
texts.text: A 'good signal' is defined as a time series signal t whose 1-day lagged series signal t -1 exhibits a high correlation with the asset's price variation ( price t -price t -1 ). The methodology we present aims to construct a linear signal that maximally captures the most of this correlation using n exogenous variables, under the constraint of a low variation of this correlation.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: In effect, µ i are estimator of E [( price t -price t -1 ) × X i,t ], and actually represents the empirical covariance between the series ( X i,t -1 ) t and the price variations ( price t -price t -1 ). The corrective term involving Σ -1 is introduced to reduce the variance of the PnL generated by this method.
texts.text: In effect, µ i are estimator of E [( price t -price t -1 ) × X i,t ], and actually represents the empirical covariance between the series ( X i,t -1 ) t and the price variations ( price t -price t -1 ). The corrective term involving Σ -1 is introduced to reduce the variance of the PnL generated by this method.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: The trade-off is mecanically set to maximize the sharpe ratio.
texts.text: The trade-off is mecanically set to maximize the sharpe ratio.

texts.label: section_header
texts.orig: Remarks:
texts.text: Remarks:

texts.label: list_item
texts.orig: · The presence of non-stationarity in the X t variable introduces bias, predominantly captured by the intercept term α 0 . Essentially, the mean contribution of each variable influences the intercept, potentially assigning it an irrelevant value that fails to capture information effectively.
texts.text: The presence of non-stationarity in the X t variable introduces bias, predominantly captured by the intercept term α 0 . Essentially, the mean contribution of each variable influences the intercept, potentially assigning it an irrelevant value that fails to capture information effectively.

texts.label: list_item
texts.orig: · Heteroscedasticity can introduce bias in the model. This phenomenon occurs because variables with larger amplitude variations are perceived by the model as being more significant compared to others. Such disproportionate emphasis on certain variables due to their variance can skew the model's performance, leading to biased outcomes.
texts.text: Heteroscedasticity can introduce bias in the model. This phenomenon occurs because variables with larger amplitude variations are perceived by the model as being more significant compared to others. Such disproportionate emphasis on certain variables due to their variance can skew the model's performance, leading to biased outcomes.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Then, a good practice is to standardize the exogenous variables X t before using them in the model, it forces them into stationnarity and homoscedasticity, at the conditio, that the mean and variance does not change too fast - i.e. the magnitude of changes is low on a timescale of 'training size'.
texts.text: Then, a good practice is to standardize the exogenous variables X t before using them in the model, it forces them into stationnarity and homoscedasticity, at the conditio, that the mean and variance does not change too fast - i.e. the magnitude of changes is low on a timescale of 'training size'.

texts.label: section_header
texts.orig: 2.1 Achieving beta neutrality
texts.text: 2.1 Achieving beta neutrality

texts.label: text
texts.orig: A limitation of this optimizatio problem is that it is not inherently beta neutral. The signal might correlate with the asset's price or, by extension, the market. However, at the expense of some performance, it is feasible to introduce a constraint to decorrelate the signal from the asset's price.
texts.text: A limitation of this optimizatio problem is that it is not inherently beta neutral. The signal might correlate with the asset's price or, by extension, the market. However, at the expense of some performance, it is feasible to introduce a constraint to decorrelate the signal from the asset's price.

texts.text: and then use this altered µ in the optimal beta formula given in section 2.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: By setting β = price t × X t , the average of the product of X t and price t , we generate a signal uncorrelated with the asset's price, achieving a form of beta neutrality. We can also choose any y t and β = y t × X t in order to get a signal uncorrelated to y t .
texts.text: By setting β = price t × X t , the average of the product of X t and price t , we generate a signal uncorrelated with the asset's price, achieving a form of beta neutrality. We can also choose any y t and β = y t × X t in order to get a signal uncorrelated to y t .

texts.orig: 2.2 Regularization techniques
texts.text: 2.2 Regularization techniques

texts.label: text
texts.orig: L1 regularization: When confronting potential redundancy among exogenous variables, L1 regularization emerges as a viable solution. It modifies the objective function to:
texts.text: L1 regularization: When confronting potential redundancy among exogenous variables, L1 regularization emerges as a viable solution. It modifies the objective function to:

texts.orig: thereby promoting sparsity in the parameter vector α . No closed-form solution is available in this case, necessitating numerical optimization to find: ˆ α = argmax ˜ L . To maintain parameter comparability and interpretability an alternative formulation can be considered:
texts.text: thereby promoting sparsity in the parameter vector α . No closed-form solution is available in this case, necessitating numerical optimization to find: ˆ α = argmax ˜ L . To maintain parameter comparability and interpretability an alternative formulation can be considered:

texts.orig: ˜ L ( α ) = L ( α ) -( λ 1 × max L ) ×| α |

texts.label: text
texts.orig: L2 regularization: For non-invertible covariance matrix Σ, L2 regularization can be utilized to ensure invertibility by adding a scaled identity matrix, resulting in:
texts.text: L2 regularization: For non-invertible covariance matrix Σ, L2 regularization can be utilized to ensure invertibility by adding a scaled identity matrix, resulting in:

texts.label: formula
texts.orig: ˜ Σ = Σ + λ 2 Id

texts.label: text
texts.orig: However, to preserve parameter comparability and interpretability, an alternative transformation can be employed:
texts.text: However, to preserve parameter comparability and interpretability, an alternative transformation can be employed:

texts.label: text
texts.orig: PCA regularization: Another approach involves the use of PCA (Principal Component Analysis) before calculating µ and Σ, in order to select only the top k principal components of ˜ X t . Caution is required: the PCA should be performed on ˜ X t rather than X t . This is because the valuable information resides in the covariance of the transformed variables ˜ X i,t = ( price t -price t -1 ) X i,t -1 , not in the covariance of the original variables. Therefore, conducting PCA within the OLS model is preferable rather than prior to it.
texts.text: PCA regularization: Another approach involves the use of PCA (Principal Component Analysis) before calculating µ and Σ, in order to select only the top k principal components of ˜ X t . Caution is required: the PCA should be performed on ˜ X t rather than X t . This is because the valuable information resides in the covariance of the transformed variables ˜ X i,t = ( price t -price t -1 ) X i,t -1 , not in the covariance of the original variables. Therefore, conducting PCA within the OLS model is preferable rather than prior to it.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Denoting µ i and σ i , the empiric mean and standard deviation of the i th principal component. Since the principals components are uncorrellated, Σ is diagonal and ˆ α is computed with :
texts.text: Denoting µ i and σ i , the empiric mean and standard deviation of the i th principal component. Since the principals components are uncorrellated, Σ is diagonal and ˆ α is computed with :

texts.label: formula

texts.label: text
texts.orig: The signal regularized is: ˆ α T Π X t with Π the ( k × n +1) matrix of the projector onto the top k principal components of ˜ X t . Note that Π is the projector onto the top k principal components of ˜ X t , not of X t .

texts.orig: Statistical Significance Regularization: For each alpha coefficient, we compute the p-value of α i and retain only those coefficients ˆ α i that meet the criterion:
texts.text: Statistical Significance Regularization: For each alpha coefficient, we compute the p-value of α i and retain only those coefficients ˆ α i that meet the criterion:

texts.label: formula
texts.orig: P [ α i = 0 | ˆ α i ] < p threshold

texts.label: text
texts.orig: The p-value is computed assuming that ˜ X t adheres to a Gaussian distribution. Under null hypothesis subsequent calculations 2 reveal that √ τ ˆ α i × ˆ α T µ follows a Student's t-distribution with τ -1 degrees of freedom, where τ denotes the length of the time series. This understanding enables the computation of the aforementioned p-value.

This understanding enables the computation of the aforementioned p-value.

texts.label: section_header
texts.orig: Practical use of the ML model
texts.text: Practical use of the ML model

texts.label: text
texts.orig: In practical terms, this model is applicable in the development of a trading strategy. This process entails the training of the model over a span of τ days, utilizing datasets ( X t -τ , ..., X t -1 ) and ( price t -τ , ..., price t -1 ). From this training dataset, ˆ α is deduced through the prescribed methodology. Then, a signal for the subsequent day, t , is formulated as ˆ α T X t .
texts.text: In practical terms, this model is applicable in the development of a trading strategy. This process entails the training of the model over a span of τ days, utilizing datasets ( X t -τ , ..., X t -1 ) and ( price t -τ , ..., price t -1 ). From this training dataset, ˆ α is deduced through the prescribed methodology. Then, a signal for the subsequent day, t , is formulated as ˆ α T X t .

texts.label: text
texts.orig: This results in a machine learning model able to at generate linear trading signals from exogenous variables. The model is parametric, encompassing parameters such as the training period τ , and a regularization parameter for each regularization technique.
texts.text: This results in a machine learning model able to at generate linear trading signals from exogenous variables. The model is parametric, encompassing parameters such as the training period τ , and a regularization parameter for each regularization technique.

texts.label: footnote
texts.orig: 2 The demonstration is straightforward in the case where Σ is diagonal because we have : √ τ ˆ α i × ˆ α T µ = √ τ µ i σ i .
texts.text: 2 The demonstration is straightforward in the case where Σ is diagonal because we have : √ τ ˆ α i × ˆ α T µ = √ τ µ i σ i .

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Remark on overfitting: With at most an order of magnitude of 5000 data points, the available dataset is quite limited, inherently posing a constant risk of overfitting. The key to the success of such a machine learning model lies in its ability to effectively implement regularization. This is the rationale behind proposing a variety of complementary regularization methods in the previous section. Beyond feature engineering, a significant portion of the empirical work presented in the next part has been dedicated to constructing and optimizing relevant regularization techniques to mitigate this issue. Still, this problem should be kept in mind as a important risk using this model for a real trading strategy.

The key to the success of such a machine learning model lies in its ability to effectively implement regularization. Beyond feature engineering, a significant portion of the empirical work presented in the next part has been dedicated to constructing and optimizing relevant regularization techniques to mitigate this issue. Still, this problem should be kept in mind as a important risk using this model for a real trading strategy.

texts.orig: 3 Empirical Application to a Trading Strategy
texts.text: 3 Empirical Application to a Trading Strategy

texts.label: text
texts.orig: The strategies empirically tested in this document is a day-to-day strategies. We consider an asset with an opening price denoted by price t , reflecting the Open price of the asset in the market. A signal signal t is generated using the proposed method. At the market opening we buy 3 , signal t shares of the asset, under the condition that the signal is sufficient, establishing a position of:
texts.text: The strategies empirically tested in this document is a day-to-day strategies. We consider an asset with an opening price denoted by price t , reflecting the Open price of the asset in the market. A signal signal t is generated using the proposed method. At the market opening we buy 3 , signal t shares of the asset, under the condition that the signal is sufficient, establishing a position of:

texts.label: formula
texts.orig: pos t = signal t × price t if: Z score ( signal t ) > 1 else: 0

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Traded Products: 'IEF' , a widely traded Exchange Traded Fund (ETF) that mirrors the performance of U.S. Treasury bonds of maturities: 1-3 years.
texts.text: Traded Products: 'IEF' , a widely traded Exchange Traded Fund (ETF) that mirrors the performance of U.S. Treasury bonds of maturities: 1-3 years.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: However, it is observed that over a longer period, we are subject to fluctuations: at times, the signal misinterprets the direction. The primary reason is that the signal can detect that the period is conducive to generating PnL, but it remains highly uncertain about which direction to take. Consequently, during each of these periods, the signal can choose the wrong direction, leading to frequent monetary losses in the strategy, which adversely affects the metrics. As a result, we obtain rather mediocre outcomes, as shown in the following figure.

As a result, we obtain rather mediocre outcomes, as shown in the following figure.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: To verify the functionality of our strategy beyond a specific set of parameters and to identify the optimal range of parameters yielding the best possible results, we conducted a comprehensive analysis by calculating the Sharpe Ratio of the PnL for various training sizes. This exercise is crucial to ensure that the strategy was not just effective for a particular set of parameters (overfitting) but also adaptable and robust across different scenarios.
texts.text: To verify the functionality of our strategy beyond a specific set of parameters and to identify the optimal range of parameters yielding the best possible results, we conducted a comprehensive analysis by calculating the Sharpe Ratio of the PnL for various training sizes. This exercise is crucial to ensure that the strategy was not just effective for a particular set of parameters (overfitting) but also adaptable and robust across different scenarios.

texts.text: Sharpe and effective sharpe ratio for different training sizes

texts.label: caption
texts.orig: Caveat: this strategy is good in terms of Sharpe ratio. Yet, when we look at the effective turnover, we realize that these strategies perform poorly from this indicator's perspective (effective turnover is more than 150%.) Indeed, when plotting the PnL curve versus the curve of positions taken, it becomes apparent that the strategy requires taking large positions to generate significant PnL.
texts.text: Caveat: this strategy is good in terms of Sharpe ratio. Yet, when we look at the effective turnover, we realize that these strategies perform poorly from this indicator's perspective (effective turnover is more than 150%.) Indeed, when plotting the PnL curve versus the curve of positions taken, it becomes apparent that the strategy requires taking large positions to generate significant PnL.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: In this specific scenario, to generate a cumulative PnL of 5 million USD, there are brief periods where an investment of up to 35 million USD is required. This observation underscores the strategy's demand for significant capital deployment at sporadic intervals to achieve the desired returns.
texts.text: In this specific scenario, to generate a cumulative PnL of 5 million USD, there are brief periods where an investment of up to 35 million USD is required. This observation underscores the strategy's demand for significant capital deployment at sporadic intervals to achieve the desired returns.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Nevertheless, given the high degree of certainty in these strategies (a Sharpe ratio of 1 implies that money is lost in less than 7% of cases), it becomes feasible to obtain such investments through leverage. This approach allows for maximizing potential returns while managing the risk associated with significant capital deployment. Thus, the described strategy either demands substantial sporadic investments, via leverage effect, or it needs to be refined through other quantitative finance methods to reduce this turnover.
texts.text: Nevertheless, given the high degree of certainty in these strategies (a Sharpe ratio of 1 implies that money is lost in less than 7% of cases), it becomes feasible to obtain such investments through leverage. texts.label: section_header
texts.orig: Conclusion
texts.text: Conclusion

texts.label: text
texts.orig: This study delineates a comprehensive framework for systematically constructing and optimizing trading strategies, anchored in a solid mathematical approach. The core innovation resides in the formulation of a signal linearly derived from selected exogenous variables. By integrating a linear combination of these variables into a strategic position-taking model, we have demonstrated a method that effectively constructs a signal responsive to market dynamics and predictive of future trends.
texts.text: This study delineates a comprehensive framework for systematically constructing and optimizing trading strategies, anchored in a solid mathematical approach. The core innovation resides in the formulation of a signal linearly derived from selected exogenous variables. By integrating a linear combination of these variables into a strategic position-taking model, we have demonstrated a method that effectively constructs a signal responsive to market dynamics and predictive of future trends.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: The methodology involved formulating the trading signal as a linear combination of a set of exogenous variables, which was subsequently employed to ascertain the position in the asset. The consequent PnL computation, based on the asset's price variations and the held positions over time, underscores the robustness of the proposed framework. This was further validated through empirical application, using publicly accessible market data, to demonstrate the efficacy of the model in constructing a durable trading strategy. Looking forward, the study opens avenues for further technological development, which include:
texts.text: The methodology involved formulating the trading signal as a linear combination of a set of exogenous variables, which was subsequently employed to ascertain the position in the asset. The consequent PnL computation, based on the asset's price variations and the held positions over time, underscores the robustness of the proposed framework. This was further validated through empirical application, using publicly accessible market data, to demonstrate the efficacy of the model in constructing a durable trading strategy. Looking forward, the study opens avenues for further technological development, which include:

texts.label: list_item
texts.orig: · Implement a New Regularization Aimed at Limiting Position Size: This approach could address the high-turnover issue observed in the empirical tests. By integrating a regularization mechanism that constraints the size of positions taken, the strategy can be fine-tuned to balance PnL generation against the risk of excessive capital allocation. It would result in a sharpe ratio / turnover trade-off. Such regularization would ensure that the strategy remains robust and efficient, without necessitating disproportionately large investments.
texts.text: Implement a New Regularization Aimed at Limiting Position Size: This approach could address the high-turnover issue observed in the empirical tests. By integrating a regularization mechanism that constraints the size of positions taken, the strategy can be fine-tuned to balance PnL generation against the risk of excessive capital allocation. It would result in a sharpe ratio / turnover trade-off. Such regularization would ensure that the strategy remains robust and efficient, without necessitating disproportionately large investments.

texts.label: list_item
texts.orig: · Adapting the Model for Long/Short Strategies: Beyond the projection method described in subsection 2.1 to achieve beta neutrality, it is feasible to adapt the model for executing long/short strategies. This requires considering a multi-layer model, where an initial signal is generated for each asset. Subsequently, one must weight the signals of each asset to maintain a balanced position situation. An analogous algorithm can be employed to optimally select this weighting.
texts.text: Adapting the Model for Long/Short Strategies: Beyond the projection method described in subsection 2.1 to achieve beta neutrality, it is feasible to adapt the model for executing long/short strategies. This requires considering a multi-layer model, where an initial signal is generated for each asset. Subsequently, one must weight the signals of each asset to maintain a balanced position situation. An analogous algorithm can be employed to optimally select this weighting.

texts.label: list_item
texts.orig: · Generalized Time Steps: In the 'How to Interpret These Results' section, we have shown that this methods constructs a time series s t such that its 1-day lagged series s t -1 is strongly correlated with the asset price variations ( price t -price t -1 ). This concept can be extended to t s -days lagged time series s t -t s , by examining their correlation with ( price t -price t -t s ). The optimal t s intervals could be determined using methods that study seasonality, such as Fourier analysis.
texts.text: Generalized Time Steps: In the 'How to Interpret These Results' section, we have shown that this methods constructs a time series s t such that its 1-day lagged series s t -1 is strongly correlated with the asset price variations ( price t -price t -1 ). This concept can be extended to t s -days lagged time series s t -t s , by examining their correlation with ( price t -price t -t s ). The optimal t s intervals could be determined using methods that study seasonality, such as Fourier analysis.

texts.label: list_item
texts.orig: · Generalized Linear Signal: Expanding the signal model to a more generalized form: signal t = f act. ( α T X t ), lead to a new expression for PnL. The samed process can be applied by optimizing the objective function L ( α ) accordingly.
texts.text: Generalized Linear Signal: Expanding the signal model to a more generalized form: signal t = f act. ( α T X t ), lead to a new expression for PnL. The samed process can be applied by optimizing the objective function L ( α ) accordingly.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Some activation function f act. such as a logit activation function inspired by Logistic Regression to create a signal that can take values in [0,1], and can be used as a mitigateur of other signals. This generalization can be explored with various activation functions used as hyperparameters.
texts.text: Some activation function f act. such as a logit activation function inspired by Logistic Regression to create a signal that can take values in [0,1], and can be used as a mitigateur of other signals. This generalization can be explored with various activation functions used as hyperparameters.

texts.label: list_item
texts.orig: · Continuous Hyperparameter Selection: In order to mitigate the risk of an overfitting signal on a hyperparameter set, we can implement a dynamic system for adjusting hyperparameters every 'training-size' days, based on the hyperparameters that maximized metrics in the previous period. Note that this bruteforce method will significantly increase the computational cost.
texts.text: Continuous Hyperparameter Selection: In order to mitigate the risk of an overfitting signal on a hyperparameter set, we can implement a dynamic system for adjusting hyperparameters every 'training-size' days, based on the hyperparameters that maximized metrics in the previous period. Note that this bruteforce method will significantly increase the computational cost.

texts.label: list_item
texts.orig: · Enhance Corrective Terms with Command & Control Theory: A primary limitation of this model is its relatively low reactivity, as it updates only every 'training-size' days. An approach grounded in command & control theory could significantly enhance the model's responsiveness, enabling real-time adjustments. The 'corrective factor' introduced in the 'Empirical Application to a Trading Strategy' section represents a rudimentary form of this adaptive correction. Investigating the application of command and control theory, especially the use of Kalman filters, to refine the model's corrective terms emerges as a promising avenue for development. Such an advancement would not only add dynamism to the model but also improve its accuracy and adaptability in response to evolving market conditions.
texts.text: Enhance Corrective Terms with Command & Control Theory: A primary limitation of this model is its relatively low reactivity, as it updates only every 'training-size' days. An approach grounded in command & control theory could significantly enhance the model's responsiveness, enabling real-time adjustments. The 'corrective factor' introduced in the 'Empirical Application to a Trading Strategy' section represents a rudimentary form of this adaptive correction. Investigating the application of command and control theory, especially the use of Kalman filters, to refine the model's corrective terms emerges as a promising avenue for development. Such an advancement would not only add dynamism to the model but also improve its accuracy and adaptability in response to evolving market conditions.

texts.label: list_item
texts.orig: · Stacking Linear Signal: Inspired by the concept of stacking regressors used in machine learning, we could create a new, stacked signal by utilizing the Optimal Linear Signal with already efficient signals used as Exogenous Variables, potentially enhancing the signals' strength.
texts.text: Stacking Linear Signal: Inspired by the concept of stacking regressors used in machine learning, we could create a new, stacked signal by utilizing the Optimal Linear Signal with already efficient signals used as Exogenous Variables, potentially enhancing the signals' strength.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: In particular, by utilizing the 'Achieving Beta Neutrality' section, one can derive a beta-neutral signal from general signals.
texts.text: In particular, by utilizing the 'Achieving Beta Neutrality' section, one can derive a beta-neutral signal from general signals.

texts.label: list_item
texts.orig: · Boosting Method: The subsection 'Achieving Beta Neutrality' develops a method to generate a signal uncorrelated to a certain time series. If a signal is generated from n variables and a PnL is deduced from it, it is entirely possible to generate a new residual signal that is uncorrelated to this previous PnL. The sum of these two signals should have been able to capture more relevant information from the variables, on the condition that some non-linearity has been introduced somewhere, and sufficient regularization avoid overfitting.
texts.text: Boosting Method: The subsection 'Achieving Beta Neutrality' develops a method to generate a signal uncorrelated to a certain time series. If a signal is generated from n variables and a PnL is deduced from it, it is entirely possible to generate a new residual signal that is uncorrelated to this previous PnL. The sum of these two signals should have been able to capture more relevant information from the variables, on the condition that some non-linearity has been introduced somewhere, and sufficient regularization avoid overfitting.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: On the same principle as Boosting in ML, by iterating each time on the residual signal (orthogonal to the previous one), it is conceivable to construct very powerful signals.
texts.text: On the same principle as Boosting in ML, by iterating each time on the residual signal (orthogonal to the previous one), it is conceivable to construct very powerful signals.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: In essence, this study not only lays a foundation for traders and financial analysts seeking to augment their strategies through quantitative methods but also sets the stage for continuous innovation and advancement in systematic trading strategies.
texts.text: In essence, this study not only lays a foundation for traders and financial analysts seeking to augment their strategies through quantitative methods but also sets the stage for continuous innovation and advancement in systematic trading strategies.
